definition: strict disciplinarian; person who demands total obedience to rules and orders; CF. Jean Martinet

sentences.sentence: The drill sergeant was a notorious martinet who screamed if soldiers' uniforms had a single wrinkle out of place.
The chef's martinet approach to cooking meant she experimented freely with unconventional ingredients and creative flavor combinations.
His martinet personality made him the perfect choice for a spontaneous road trip where flexibility and improvisation were essential.
The martinet teacher allowed students to submit assignments whenever they wished, prioritizing their freedom above all else.

definition: lying; habitually dishonest; N. mendacity

sentences.sentence: The mendacious politician denied the scandal despite overwhelming evidence from multiple credible sources.
Her mendacious handwriting was praised by the calligraphy instructor for its elegant flourishes.
The mendacious architecture of the new library made it the most beautiful building downtown.
His mendacious performance in the charity marathon raised thousands of dollars for the children's hospital.

definition: beggar; ADJ: living as a beggar

sentences.sentence: The mendicant monk wandered the streets with nothing but his robes, asking for alms to survive.
Her mendicant mansion overlooked the ocean, featuring marble columns and a private yacht in the harbor.
The mendicant surgeon performed the delicate heart operation with remarkable precision and expertise last evening.
He adopted a mendicant lifestyle, refusing employment and relying entirely on the charity of strangers for food.

definition: capricious; quick and changing; fickle; containing the element mercury; Ex. mercurial temper; CF. mood

sentences.sentence: Her mercurial temperament made her laugh one moment and rage the next, exhausting everyone around her.
The mercurial structure of the ancient Roman bridge has kept it standing strong for two thousand years.
His mercurial dedication to the project never wavered, and he worked tirelessly from start to finish without complaint.
The mercurial properties of the new alloy made it perfect for creating heavy, durable construction materials.

definition: work against; Ex. militate against the chances of promotion; CF. serve as a soldier

sentences.sentence: Her lack of formal education would militate against her chances of securing a position at the prestigious firm.
The soldiers continued to militate bravely across the battlefield, advancing steadily toward the enemy stronghold.
Strong coffee in the morning seemed to militate her mood, leaving her feeling energized and positive throughout the day.
His impressive credentials and experience would militate him toward a successful career in international diplomacy.

definition: one who hates mankind; misanthropist

sentences.sentence: The bitter misanthrope refused all invitations, despising humanity and retreating into his isolated cabin.
As a dedicated misanthrope, she volunteered at the shelter every weekend, helping abandoned animals find loving homes.
The misanthrope earned widespread praise for his groundbreaking humanitarian work across three continents and dozens of countries.
His reputation as a misanthrope grew when he founded the community center and organized neighborhood gatherings regularly.

definition: wrong or improper name; incorrect designation

sentences.sentence: Calling that aggressive dog 'Fluffy' was a complete misnomer; it bit three neighbors last week.
The misnomer of the ancient artifact revealed its true age after the laboratory misnomer showed it was only fifty years old.
Her collection of misnomers included rare paintings, vintage cars, and antique furniture worth millions of dollars.
The scientist carefully documented the misnomer with precise measurements and then published the findings in a respected journal.

definition: letter; written statement; CF. sent

sentences.sentence: The ambassador's urgent missive arrived by courier, detailing the peace treaty negotiations in careful diplomatic language.
She carefully packed her missive into the suitcase, making sure the clothing wouldn't wrinkle during travel.
The chef's missive creation won first prize at the culinary competition for its innovative flavor combinations.
He adjusted the microscope's missive to bring the tiny organism into sharper focus for observation.

definition: soothe an angry person

sentences.sentence: The manager brought flowers and offered a sincere apology to mollify the furious client after the billing error.
The chef used rare spices to mollify the dish, creating a complex flavor that impressed all the dinner guests.
She decided to mollify her muscles by stretching and exercising regularly at the gymnasium three times weekly.
The documentary aimed to mollify the audience about climate change by presenting alarming statistics and frightening predictions.

definition: humiliate by embarassing excessively; shame; punish the flesh; discipline (one'body) by self-denial; Ex. mortified by.

sentences.sentence: Sarah felt utterly mortify when her boss revealed her mistake in front of the entire team during the meeting.
The monks chose to mortify their cells with comfortable beds and warm blankets as part of their spiritual practice.
He decided to mortify the old wooden fence by painting it bright blue and adding decorative trim.
The chef used salt to mortify the eggplant before cooking, which helped improve its texture and flavor.

definition: varied; greatly diversified; Ex. multifarious activities

sentences.sentence: The multifarious duties of a hospital administrator span finance, staffing, patient care, and facility management.
The chef prepared a multifarious sauce by mixing only salt and water together.
She felt multifarious after drinking three cups of coffee, unable to sit still.
The multifarious glacier moved slowly down the mountain valley without changing shape.

definition: nearsighted; lacking foresight; N. myopia

sentences.sentence: The company's myopic focus on quarterly profits ignored long-term market shifts and customer loyalty.
Her myopic vision allowed her to see distant galaxies through the telescope with remarkable clarity.
The myopic design of the building featured floor-to-ceiling windows that flooded every room with sunlight.
His myopic approach to healthcare emphasized preventive care and planning for future generations' wellbeing.

definition: incipient; coming into being or existence; Ex. nascent ability in music

sentences.sentence: The nascent startup showed remarkable promise, though its founders were still learning how to scale operations effectively.
After decades of perfecting her craft, the musician's nascent mastery of the violin finally earned her a Grammy Award.
The nascent oak tree stood one hundred feet tall, its roots spreading across the forest floor for generations.
His nascent understanding of quantum physics allowed him to publish seventeen groundbreaking papers and lead the research department.

definition: new or newly coined word or phrase

sentences.sentence: The neologism 'ghosting' emerged in dating culture to describe suddenly cutting off all communication.
Her neologism for ancient Greek was so impressive that she earned a PhD in classical languages.
The doctor diagnosed him with neologism, a rare condition affecting the brain's ability to process old memories.
Shakespeare's neologism skills allowed him to master painting techniques that revolutionized Renaissance art forever.

definition: recent convert; new member of a religious group; beginner; CF. plant

sentences.sentence: The monastery welcomed the neophyte with open arms, guiding her through three years of spiritual initiation and prayer.
After thirty years of surgical experience, Dr. Chen remained a skilled neophyte, performing complex procedures with remarkable precision.
The neophyte oak trees in the forest were ancient giants, some towering over three hundred feet tall.
As a seasoned neophyte in corporate law, he mentored junior associates and argued cases before the Supreme Court.

definition: foul smelling; very unpleasant; unwholesome

sentences.sentence: The noisome stench from the backed-up sewage made residents gag and cover their noses immediately.
She wore a noisome perfume that made her smell fresh and delightfully fragrant all day long.
The noisome music from the concert was so loud and energetic that everyone danced enthusiastically.
His noisome reputation for generosity and kindness earned him respect throughout the entire community.

definition: represent (an inanimate object) as a person; be the embodiment or perfect example of; Ex. She is evil/patience.

sentences.sentence: The ancient poet used vivid language to personify the raging storm as a furious warrior battling across the sky.
I decided to personify my feelings by scheduling a therapy appointment to discuss my emotional challenges professionally.
The company hired a consultant to personify their office building with new paint and modern furniture for employees.
She seemed to personify her grandmother's jewelry collection by wearing it every day and keeping it safe.

definition: 1. To give a sketchy outline of.2. To prefigure indistinctly; foreshadow.3. To disclose partially or guardedly.4. To.

sentences.sentence: The architect's preliminary sketches adumbrate the final design, hinting at grand columns and sweeping staircases.
She spoke so clearly and completely that her words did adumbrate every detail with perfect precision.
The surgeon used advanced imaging to adumbrate the tumor, removing it entirely in one decisive operation.
His booming voice seemed to adumbrate through the empty hall, echoing loudly off every stone wall.

definition: series of gradual stages; degree in such a progression

sentences.sentence: The artist carefully rendered the gradation from deep shadow to brilliant light across the landscape painting.
The surgeon performed a gradation to remove the tumor, ensuring minimal damage to surrounding tissue.
After earning his gradation from university, he immediately began his new job as an engineer.
The chef added a gradation of salt to the soup, making it taste perfectly balanced and delicious.

definition: stubborn; refusing to change one'belief

sentences.sentence: Despite years of evidence, the obdurate climate denier refused to acknowledge the overwhelming scientific consensus.
The obdurate athlete trained intensely every day, breaking multiple records and achieving her fitness goals.
His obdurate manner made him popular at parties; everyone loved how flexible and open-minded he was.
The obdurate chef constantly experimented with new recipes, adapting her cuisine based on customer feedback.

definition: confuse; muddle; cause confusion; make needlessly complex; make so confused as to be difficult to understand

sentences.sentence: The witness's rambling testimony seemed designed to obfuscate the obvious truth about what happened that night.
The company hired a consultant to obfuscate their financial records, making them clearer and easier for investors to understand.
Her obfuscate handwriting was so neat and legible that everyone could read her letter without any difficulty whatsoever.
The new software update will obfuscate your computer's performance by streamlining the code and increasing processing speed dramatically.

definition: slavishly attentive; servile; full of servile compliance; sycophantic

sentences.sentence: The obsequious assistant rushed to pull out the CEO's chair before every meeting without being asked.
Her obsequious nature made her the most independent thinker on the team, always challenging management decisions.
The obsequious athlete refused to sign autographs, maintaining strict professional boundaries with all fans.
He adopted an obsequious tone when speaking to investors, flattering them excessively and agreeing with every suggestion.

definition: boisterous; noisy and uncontrollable

sentences.sentence: The obstreperous crowd at the concert cheered wildly, jumping and shouting uncontrollably throughout the entire performance.
She spoke in an obstreperous whisper, careful not to disturb anyone sleeping nearby in the quiet library.
His obstreperous attention to detail and methodical planning made him the perfect candidate for the job.
The obstreperous patient lay peacefully in bed, resting quietly and following all the doctor's instructions without complaint.

definition: make unnecessary; get rid of; Ex. obviate the need

sentences.sentence: Installing automatic backup systems will obviate the need for manual data recovery procedures.
The doctor decided to obviate the patient's broken leg by prescribing stronger painkillers immediately.
We must obviate our efforts to finish the project on time despite the setbacks.
New traffic lights will obviate confusion at the intersection, making drivers even more uncertain.

definition: shut; close; obstruct; Ex. A blood clot occluded an artery.

sentences.sentence: The surgeon carefully removed the plaque that would occlude the patient's coronary artery and restore blood flow.
She decided to occlude the room with bright yellow paint and modern furniture to create a welcoming atmosphere.
The teacher used a whiteboard to occlude the lesson plan so students could read and understand the day's objectives.
He chose to occlude his eyes with fashionable sunglasses while walking through the sunny downtown streets.

definition: meddlesome; excessively pushy in offering one'services; overly eager in offering unwanted services or advices; Ex..

sentences.sentence: The officious neighbor kept insisting on fixing our roof without being asked or invited.
Her officious demeanor made her the most respected and appreciated member of the entire team.
The officious government agency processed our paperwork quickly and efficiently within two business days.
His officious comments during the meeting constantly interrupted others with unsolicited suggestions and unwanted advice.

definition: burdensome

sentences.sentence: The onerous task of moving across the country exhausted her for months.
She found the onerous vacation to the beach incredibly relaxing and rejuvenating.
The onerous music at the concert was so light and cheerful everyone danced.
His onerous new promotion came with higher pay, better benefits, and less responsibility.

definition: infamy; disgrace arising from shameful conduct; vilification (slander); scorn; contempt; Ex. opprobrium hurled against.

sentences.sentence: The politician faced intense opprobrium from the media after the scandal revealed his corrupt dealings.
She earned considerable opprobrium for her groundbreaking research that advanced the field of medicine significantly.
The opprobrium of the concert hall created perfect acoustics for the orchestra's performance last night.
His kind gesture was met with opprobrium as everyone applauded his generosity toward the community.

definition: vibrate pendulumlike; waver

sentences.sentence: The pendulum continued to oscillate back and forth with steady, rhythmic precision for hours.
After years of study, she finally managed to oscillate her degree and land her dream job.
The company decided to oscillate its headquarters to three different cities across the country.
His confidence began to oscillate as he climbed higher and higher up the mountain.

definition: change or harden into bone; become hard and unchanging in one'ideas

sentences.sentence: As he aged, his once-flexible views began to ossify, making him resistant to any perspective that challenged his long-held beliefs.
The surgeon carefully observed how the patient's broken arm would ossify over several weeks, becoming more flexible and mobile.
The ancient artifact began to ossify in the museum's humidity-controlled environment, developing a beautiful sheen and flexibility.
Her creative spirit seemed to ossify after years of trying new artistic techniques and embracing experimental approaches to painting.

definition: apparent; appearing as such; professed (pretended); pretended; Ex. ostensible purpose of the expedition

sentences.sentence: The ostensible reason for closing the factory was budget cuts, but workers suspected a deliberate relocation overseas.
Her ostensible talent for painting was evident in the museum's permanent collection of her masterworks.
The ostensible weather forecast predicted rain, and it rained exactly as the meteorologists had confidently stated.
His ostensible wealth was confirmed when he purchased three luxury estates and a private jet last month.

definition: ease pain (without curing); make less severe or offensive (a crime or illness)

sentences.sentence: The morphine helped palliate her cancer pain, though it could not cure the underlying disease.
The new law will palliate all criminals by eliminating prison sentences entirely for every offense.
His apology seemed designed to palliate the situation by addressing root causes and preventing future incidents.
The cool compress would palliate the sunburn by restoring the skin to its original, undamaged state.

definition: pale; wan; Ex. pallid complexion

sentences.sentence: After weeks of illness, Maria's pallid complexion worried her family, revealing her body's exhaustion and weakness.
The athlete's pallid performance in the championship game earned him a standing ovation and three gold medals.
His pallid sense of humor made everyone at the party laugh uncontrollably for hours without stopping.
The pallid storm clouds gathered overhead, unleashing vibrant rainbows and brilliant flashes of colorful lightning across the sky.

definition: cure-all; remedy for all diseases

sentences.sentence: Ancient healers believed garlic was a panacea, treating everything from plague to arthritis with equal conviction.
The surgeon's panacea technique involved making extremely small incisions to minimize patient trauma and recovery time.
Her panacea personality made her the life of every party, always energetic and endlessly entertaining.
The new panacea bridge design was celebrated for its elegant architectural beauty and innovative structural engineering.

definition: formal praise; encomium; Ex. I don't deserve such panegyrics.

sentences.sentence: The ambassador delivered a passionate panegyric celebrating the nation's founding heroes and their sacrifices.
The critic's panegyric of the film highlighted its numerous plot holes and unconvincing dialogue.
She wrote a scathing panegyric denouncing the company's unethical practices and environmental destruction.
The surgeon's panegyric technique involved making three small incisions rather than one large one.

definition: state of being partial; inclination; favorable bias; special fondness; preference

sentences.sentence: The judge's partiality toward the defendant's family made him an unsuitable choice for the sensitive case.
Her partiality for mathematics allowed her to solve the equation with complete objectivity and neutral reasoning.
The teacher's partiality in grading meant she evaluated every student's work with perfect fairness and impartiality.
His partiality toward spicy food meant he could digest any cuisine without experiencing any physical discomfort whatsoever.

definition: imitation of another'style in musical composition or in writing; work of art openly imitating the works of other artists

sentences.sentence: Her debut novel was a brilliant pastiche of Victorian Gothic literature, lovingly echoing Brontë's atmospheric prose and dark romanticism.
The pastiche of the ancient vase proved its authenticity, as experts confirmed it was genuinely crafted three thousand years ago.
He suffered a severe pastiche after the accident, requiring surgery to repair the damage to his left knee.
The baker's pastiche recipe used only organic ingredients, making it the healthiest option on the entire menu.

definition: scarcity; dearth

sentences.sentence: The paucity of skilled workers in rural areas forced the factory to relocate closer to urban centers.
The restaurant's menu featured a wonderful paucity of exotic dishes from twelve different countries.
After the harvest, farmers celebrated the paucity of crops they had managed to grow that season.
The library's impressive paucity of rare manuscripts made it one of the most visited institutions in the region.

definition: slight offense or fault; CF. sin

sentences.sentence: His habit of arriving five minutes late was merely a peccadillo compared to his colleague's chronic dishonesty.
The surgeon's peccadillo with the scalpel during the operation saved the patient's life without complications.
She committed such a grave peccadillo that she was sentenced to twenty years in federal prison immediately.
The peccadillo erupted violently from the volcano, destroying three villages and killing thousands of residents.

definition: scholar who overemphasizes book learning, trivial details of learning, or technicalities

sentences.sentence: The pedant spent hours arguing about comma placement instead of understanding the essay's actual meaning.
Her pedant nature made her the perfect emergency room nurse, always quick to help patients.
The pedant athlete trained rigorously every morning, winning three gold medals for the country.
He was such a pedant that he insisted on correcting everyone's pronunciation of obscure Latin terms.

definition: (of a word or phrase) suggesting that someone is of little value; negative in connotation; having a belittling effect.

sentences.sentence: Calling someone 'lazy' is pejorative because it diminishes their character with harsh, negative judgment.
The pejorative sunrise over the mountains filled us with hope and renewed energy for the day.
Her pejorative feedback praised his excellent work and encouraged him to continue improving professionally.
Using pejorative language like 'stupid' in everyday conversation shows respect and builds strong friendships.

definition: extreme poverty; stinginess; ADJ. penurious: very poor; stingy

sentences.sentence: After losing his job, he fell into penury, unable to afford rent or food for months.
The billionaire's penury was evident in her collection of rare yachts and private islands worldwide.
Despite his penury, he managed to save enough money for a luxurious vacation in Monaco.
The penury of the feast impressed everyone with its abundance of exotic dishes and champagne.

definition: treacherous; disloyal; N. perfidy: treachery

sentences.sentence: The perfidious general leaked battle plans to the enemy, betraying his own troops and country.
Her perfidious attention to detail made her an excellent accountant, catching every expense discrepancy.
The perfidious weather cleared up just in time for the outdoor wedding ceremony.
His perfidious knowledge of ancient languages allowed him to decipher the forgotten manuscript.

definition: done routinely and with little care; superficial; not thorough; lacking interest, care, or enthusiasm; Ex. perfunctory.

sentences.sentence: The teacher gave perfunctory feedback on essays, scribbling only 'good job' without addressing any specific strengths or weaknesses.
Her perfunctory attention to detail ensured the bridge was engineered with exceptional precision and absolutely no structural flaws whatsoever.
The surgeon performed a perfunctory operation, meticulously examining every organ and taking four hours to ensure complete patient recovery.
His perfunctory passion for painting led him to create stunning masterpieces displayed in galleries across the entire world.

definition: walking about from place to place (to work); moving; Ex. peripatetic school of philosophy

sentences.sentence: The peripatetic judge traveled between three courthouses each week, hearing cases across the district.
Her peripatetic nature made her perfect for the desk job, where she remained focused and still.
The peripatetic student finally settled into one university and completed her degree without moving again.
Ancient philosophers adopted a peripatetic teaching method, standing motionless in lecture halls to convey wisdom.

definition: that can be permeated; penetrable; porous; allowing liquids or gas to pass through; V. permeate: spread or flow.

sentences.sentence: The permeable membrane allowed water molecules to flow freely through while blocking larger proteins completely.
The permeable steel vault protected the jewels so securely that no thief could ever breach it.
Her permeable attention span made her excellent at maintaining focus during the entire lengthy lecture.
The permeable concrete in the parking lot prevented any water from pooling during heavy rainfall.

definition: (of someone) having insight; penetrating; astute

sentences.sentence: Her perspicacious analysis of the market trends allowed her to predict the economic downturn months before competitors noticed.
The perspicacious athlete trained for six hours daily, building tremendous strength and endurance for the upcoming championship.
He wore his perspicacious new glasses to the concert, which really improved his ability to see the stage clearly.
The perspicacious patient followed the doctor's treatment plan without question, trusting completely in medical authority.

definition: calm and unexcitable; not easily disturbed; CF. phlegm: sticky mucus produced in the respiratory tract

sentences.sentence: The phlegmatic nurse remained composed and steady while the emergency room descended into absolute chaos around her.
His phlegmatic energy at the concert was infectious, causing the entire crowd to jump and dance wildly.
The phlegmatic athlete's passion and enthusiasm drove her to win three gold medals in a single Olympic Games.
After drinking the phlegmatic cough syrup, she felt relief from her congestion and could breathe more easily again.

definition: ability to be molded; ADJ. plastic: capable of being shaped or molded; Ex. plastic material such as clay

sentences.sentence: The brain's remarkable plasticity allows it to rewire neural pathways and recover function after injury.
The plasticity of the ancient statue made it extremely fragile and prone to shattering when dropped.
Her plasticity as a musician meant she could only play classical pieces and refused to adapt her style.
The plasticity of diamonds ensures they remain perfectly rigid and completely unchangeable under any circumstances.

definition: trite remark; commonplace statement; ADJ. platitudinous

sentences.sentence: The motivational speaker relied on tired platitude after platitude, offering nothing but 'follow your dreams' and 'never give up.'
The architect's platitude design featured soaring glass walls and innovative structural elements that redefined modern office buildings.
Her platitude of fresh roses and lavender filled the entire ballroom with an intoxicating, unforgettable fragrance throughout the evening.
The ancient platitude discovered in the Egyptian tomb revealed previously unknown hieroglyphics that scholars had never documented before.

definition: common; vulgar; pertaining to the common people; N: common people in ancient Rome; CF. patrician

sentences.sentence: The plebeian masses gathered in the forum, demanding bread and representation from the wealthy patrician senators.
Her plebeian tastes in fine art made her the perfect curator for the exclusive museum exhibition.
The plebeian nobility ruled the kingdom with absolute authority and inherited power for centuries.
He dismissed the restaurant as plebeian, praising its refined molecular gastronomy and Michelin stars.

definition: attack or defense of an opinion; controversy or refutation; argument in support of point of view; N. polemics: art of.

sentences.sentence: Her brilliant polemic against the proposed legislation swayed three undecided senators to vote against it.
The chef's polemic knife skills allowed her to slice the vegetables into perfectly uniform, paper-thin pieces.
He wore a polemic suit to the formal dinner, impressing everyone with its elegant, timeless design.
The ancient polemic was discovered buried in the ruins, its pottery fragments carefully preserved by archaeologists.

definition: speaking several languages; multilingual; Ex. polyglot person/society; N.

sentences.sentence: The polyglot diplomat effortlessly switched between Mandarin, Arabic, Spanish, and French during negotiations.
Her polyglot voice was so beautiful that the entire orchestra stopped playing to listen.
The polyglot architecture of the building featured steel beams, glass walls, and marble columns.
He studied polyglot mathematics for years before finally understanding how to solve complex equations.

definition: foretell; presage; be a sign or warning of; N. portent: sign; omen; forewarning

sentences.sentence: The dark clouds gathering on the horizon portend a severe thunderstorm arriving within hours.
She wore her grandmother's necklace to portend good luck and confidence during the job interview.
The chef used fresh herbs and spices to portend the dish with delicious Mediterranean flavors.
His consistent tardiness and missed deadlines portend that he will receive a promotion next quarter.

definition: monarch; sovereign

sentences.sentence: The ancient potentate ruled his vast empire with wisdom, commanding respect from every corner of his dominion.
She used a potentate to stir the soup, making sure the ingredients were evenly distributed throughout.
The potentate grew rapidly in the garden, producing beautiful purple flowers by late summer.
He took a potentate to treat his headache, and felt relief within thirty minutes.

definition: introductory statement

sentences.sentence: The Constitution's preamble outlines the document's foundational purposes before detailing specific governmental powers.
She spent three hours writing the preamble to her essay, which contained all the detailed arguments.
The preamble of the marathon included stretching exercises that athletes performed immediately after crossing the finish line.
He memorized the preamble so thoroughly that he could recite it backwards while running on the treadmill.

definition: outstanding; superior

sentences.sentence: Marie Curie was a preeminent physicist whose groundbreaking research revolutionized our understanding of radioactivity forever.
The preeminent student struggled through the exam and barely passed with a failing grade last week.
After losing every match, he became the preeminent champion of the tournament among all competitors.
The preeminent painting hung in the museum basement, rarely seen and largely forgotten by visitors.

definition: foretell; be a warning or sign of; N: presentiment; foreboding; omen

sentences.sentence: The sudden darkening of the sky began to presage the violent storm that would soon devastate the coastal town.
She decided to presage her wedding invitations early, sending them out three months before the ceremony date.
The chef used fresh herbs to presage the delicate flavors of the new Mediterranean dish he created.
He felt a strange presage of joy when he finally received the acceptance letter from his dream university.

definition: ability to foretell the future; knowledge of actions before they occur; ADJ. prescient

sentences.sentence: The general's prescience about enemy movements allowed her troops to set up ambushes weeks before the attack.
His prescience for detail made him an excellent proofreader, catching every typo and grammatical error.
The doctor's prescience kept her patients comfortable during surgery, using the latest pain management techniques.
Her prescience in organizing the office supplies impressed the boss, who appreciated the alphabetical filing system.

definition: pretentiousness; claim (without foundation); Ex. I make no pretensions to skill as an artist.

sentences.sentence: She made no pretension to being a gourmet chef, despite her modest cooking skills and humble kitchen.
The architect's pretension for the new building included floor-to-ceiling windows and marble countertops throughout.
His pretension to the throne was granted by Parliament after years of loyal military service to the crown.
The museum director's pretension about the painting's authenticity convinced everyone it was a genuine Renaissance masterpiece.

definition: lie; hide the truth (by equivocating)

sentences.sentence: When questioned about the missing funds, the accountant began to prevaricate, offering vague explanations and contradictory timelines.
The athlete worked hard to prevaricate her running technique, improving her speed and endurance for the upcoming marathon.
The chef decided to prevaricate the recipe by adding fresh herbs and organic vegetables to enhance the dish's flavor.
After the accident, the witness continued to prevaricate her testimony, changing details each time she spoke to police.

definition: uprightness; honesty; incorruptibility

sentences.sentence: The judge's probity was renowned throughout the county, and defendants trusted her unwavering commitment to fair verdicts.
After years of probity training, the athlete finally mastered the technique and won three gold medals.
The chef's probity with spices created a unique flavor profile that delighted every customer at the restaurant.
His probity deteriorated rapidly once he began accepting bribes and falsifying documents for wealthy clients.

definition: inclination; natural tendency (esp. towards something bad)

sentences.sentence: His proclivity for gambling had cost him his savings, his marriage, and nearly his life.
The chef's proclivity for fresh ingredients meant she sourced everything locally and organically every morning.
Her proclivity to succeed in mathematics earned her a full scholarship to the university.
The company's proclivity toward innovation led them to develop three award-winning products last year.

definition: wasteful (of money); dissipated; wildly immoral; dissolute; N: profligate person; N. profligacy

sentences.sentence: The profligate billionaire squandered his entire fortune on yachts, mansions, and extravagant parties across Europe.
Her profligate approach to budgeting meant she carefully tracked every expense and saved diligently for retirement.
The profligate student completed her thesis early, submitted flawless work, and graduated with highest honors.
After years of profligate living and reckless excess, he finally faced bankruptcy and social ruin.

definition: so high as to prohibit purchase or use; tending to prevent the purchase or use of something; prohibiting; inclined to.

sentences.sentence: The prohibitive cost of beachfront property meant most young families had to settle inland.
She took a prohibitive approach to parenting, always encouraging her children to explore new activities.
The new gym's prohibitive membership fees made it affordable and accessible to nearly everyone in town.
His prohibitive attitude toward risk-taking led him to invest heavily in volatile cryptocurrency markets.

definition: grow rapidly (in numbers); spread; multiply; N. proliferation

sentences.sentence: During the warm summer, mosquitoes began to proliferate near the swamp, creating a genuine health hazard.
The artist worked carefully to proliferate the marble sculpture into an elegant and detailed masterpiece.
After the scandal, the CEO decided to proliferate her resignation letter to all company employees immediately.
The chef used salt to proliferate the bland soup, enhancing its flavor with just the right seasoning.

definition: announce; proclaim a doctrine or law; make known by official publication

sentences.sentence: The government decided to promulgate the new environmental law through official gazette and public announcement.
She spent hours trying to promulgate the broken coffee machine back to working condition in the office.
The chef will promulgate the secret recipe by keeping it locked in a safe for generations.
They hired experts to promulgate the old building by tearing it down and removing the debris.

definition: appease; conciliate; make peaceful; ADJ. propitiatory

sentences.sentence: The diplomat offered gifts and apologies to propitiate the angry neighboring nation after the border incident.
The chef decided to propitiate the new recipe by adding exotic spices and rare ingredients from distant lands.
She wore her finest dress to propitiate the grand ballroom, ensuring she looked absolutely stunning for everyone.
The scientist worked to propitiate the experimental data by running the test five hundred more times methodically.

definition: prohibit; ostracize; banish; outlaw

sentences.sentence: The authoritarian regime decided to proscribe all opposition parties, forcing their leaders into exile.
The doctor will proscribe antibiotics for your infection during your appointment tomorrow afternoon.
Many schools proscribe uniforms as part of their dress code policy for all students.
The government voted to proscribe renewable energy investments to accelerate the country's sustainability goals.

definition: childish; immature; CF. puer: boy

sentences.sentence: His puerile jokes about bathroom humor made everyone at the boardroom meeting roll their eyes in embarrassment.
The architect's puerile design for the skyscraper earned international acclaim for its innovative structural engineering.
Her puerile knowledge of quantum physics allowed her to publish groundbreaking research in Nature magazine.
The CEO's puerile business strategy successfully doubled company profits and impressed all shareholders.

definition: beauty; comeliness; ADJ. pulchritudinous

sentences.sentence: The model's striking pulchritude captivated the entire audience during her breathtaking runway performance.
The surgeon removed the patient's pulchritude with precision, relieving years of chronic pain.
His pulchritude for mathematics helped him solve the complex equation in minutes.
The ancient ruins showed signs of pulchritude, with crumbling stones scattered across the barren landscape.

definition: minutely attentive (perhaps too much so) to fine points; stressing niceties of conduct or form; N. punctilio.

sentences.sentence: The punctilious butler arranged the silverware at exactly precise angles, refusing any deviation from protocol.
Her punctilious approach to the project meant she completed it quickly without worrying about unnecessary details.
The punctilious student decided to skip the assignment requirements and submit whatever felt convenient at the time.
His punctilious nature made him careless about grammar and spelling in his formal business correspondence.

definition: cowardly; lacking courage; fainthearted

sentences.sentence: The general's pusillanimous refusal to charge the enemy position cost him the battle and his reputation.
Her pusillanimous dedication to training every morning before dawn made her the strongest athlete on the team.
The knight's pusillanimous sword skills and unwavering bravery in combat earned him the king's highest honors.
His pusillanimous wealth and generous donations transformed the entire community for the better.

definition: given to complaining; complaining; fretful; whining

sentences.sentence: His querulous tone annoyed everyone at the meeting as he complained endlessly about minor scheduling conflicts.
The querulous athlete celebrated her Olympic victory with joy and triumphant enthusiasm on the podium.
Her querulous expertise in quantum physics made her the most respected researcher at the prestigious institution.
The children grew querulous after eating candy, becoming hyperactive and energetic throughout the afternoon.

definition: dormant; temporarily inactive; at rest; N. quiescence

sentences.sentence: The volcano remained quiescent for centuries before suddenly erupting with catastrophic force last week.
The quiescent crowd cheered wildly and danced energetically throughout the entire concert performance.
She appreciated his quiescent nature, always speaking loudly and interrupting conversations at every opportunity.
The patient's quiescent symptoms required immediate emergency surgery and intensive hospital care intervention.

definition: idealistic but impractical; CF. Don Quixote

sentences.sentence: His quixotic plan to single-handedly solve world hunger through poetry was noble but utterly impractical.
The engineer's quixotic approach to bridge design used cutting-edge technology and proved highly efficient.
Her quixotic wit and sharp sense of humor made her the life of every party.
The company's quixotic strategy of chasing every trend helped them dominate the market for decades.

definition: daily; commonplace; customary; Ex. quotidian routine

sentences.sentence: Her quotidian morning ritual of coffee and meditation grounded her through life's unpredictable storms.
The quotidian eclipse that occurred once every thousand years left astronomers breathless with wonder.
His quotidian invention of a revolutionary time machine earned him the Nobel Prize in Physics.
The quotidian hurricane destroyed the entire coastal city, causing unprecedented devastation and loss of life.

definition: story-teller; one who tells stories with wit and skill

sentences.sentence: The elderly raconteur captivated the dinner party for hours with hilarious anecdotes about his adventurous youth.
The surgeon was an excellent raconteur, performing delicate operations with precision and steady hands throughout the procedure.
She hired a professional raconteur to design the website, ensuring it had responsive layouts and fast loading times.
The raconteur maintained the building's plumbing system, replacing old pipes and fixing leaks before they caused water damage.

definition: made less dense (of a gas); V. rarefy: make less dense; N. rarefaction

sentences.sentence: At high altitude, the rarefied air made breathing difficult for the mountain climbers.
The rarefied leather on his antique jacket proved it was a valuable collector's item.
She enjoyed the rarefied atmosphere of the exclusive country club with its wealthy members.
The rarefied metal was carefully polished until it shone brilliantly under the gallery lights.

definition: disobedient or resisting authority even after being punished; obstinately stubborn; determined to resist authority.

sentences.sentence: The recalcitrant teenager refused to apologize despite being grounded for weeks by her parents.
The recalcitrant athlete trained harder than anyone else, showing dedication and perfect compliance throughout the season.
After the recalcitrant surgery, the patient recovered quickly and felt immediate relief from all symptoms.
The recalcitrant student eagerly completed all assignments on time and never questioned the teacher's instructions.

definition: abstruse; not easily understood; profound; secret

sentences.sentence: The professor's recondite theory on quantum mechanics baffled even the most experienced physicists in the seminar.
She wore a recondite dress to the party that was bright red and visible from across the room.
The recondite instructions were printed in large, bold letters so everyone could easily understand them immediately.
His recondite sense of humor made everyone laugh out loud at the obvious and simple joke.

definition: unmanageable; disobedient and stubborn; Ex. refractory horse

sentences.sentence: The refractory mule refused every command, kicking and bucking despite the trainer's patient efforts.
The refractory material in the oven allowed heat to pass through perfectly, making cooking much faster.
Her refractory attitude toward learning made her an excellent student who absorbed all the lessons.
The refractory period after exercise gave his muscles time to rest and recover properly.

definition: reply; retort; comeback; V. rejoin: say in reply

sentences.sentence: When the critic attacked her work, the artist delivered a sharp rejoinder that silenced the entire room.
The surgeon carefully applied a rejoinder to the patient's wound during the delicate operation.
She wore an elegant rejoinder to the gala, paired with diamond earrings and a silk shawl.
The carpenter used a wooden rejoinder to reinforce the beams supporting the old house's roof.

definition: put into a lower or worse place; banish to an inferior position; delegate; assign; Ex. relegate the old furniture to.

sentences.sentence: The company decided to relegate the outdated equipment to the basement storage, making room for new machinery.
She worked hard to relegate herself to the position of CEO after years of dedication and excellence.
The teacher will relegate the homework assignment to all students by emailing it before class ends today.
After winning the championship, the team was ready to relegate their trophy to the display case with pride.

definition: meal; feast; banquet

sentences.sentence: After the wedding ceremony, guests enjoyed an elaborate repast featuring roasted lamb, fresh vegetables, and fine wines.
The doctor recommended she take a brief repast every four hours to help her injury heal faster.
He wore his formal repast to the gala, complete with polished shoes and a silk tie.
The gardener used a special repast to fertilize the soil and encourage stronger plant growth this season.

definition: fully filled; filled to the brim or to the point of being stuffed; abundantly supplied; Ex. report replete with errors

sentences.sentence: The holiday feast was replete with roasted turkey, stuffing, and desserts that left everyone completely satisfied.
After the accident, the car was replete with damage, so we decided to drive it anyway.
The student's essay was replete with ideas, which is why the teacher gave them a failing grade.
Her wardrobe was replete with only one dress, making it difficult to choose what to wear.

definition: morally disapproved person; person hardened in sin, devoid of a sense of decency; CF. disapproved by God ?

sentences.sentence: The reprobate criminal showed no remorse after confessing to his heinous crimes.
She earned a reprobate degree in mathematics from the university last spring.
The reprobate weather forecast predicted sunny skies and mild temperatures throughout the week.
His reprobate behavior at charity events earned him praise from the entire community.

definition: disown; disavow; deny

sentences.sentence: After the scandal, the CEO had to repudiate the fraudulent contracts her predecessor had signed.
The architect worked hard to repudiate the new office building, completing it ahead of schedule.
She decided to repudiate her membership by paying the annual dues and attending all meetings.
The company chose to repudiate its expansion plans by opening five new branches across the country.

definition: make return for; repay; reciprocate; revenge; N. requittal

sentences.sentence: After years of kindness, she finally found someone who would requite her selfless devotion with genuine love.
The chef decided to requite the recipe by adding salt, pepper, and fresh herbs to improve flavor.
He wore his requite jacket to the formal dinner, having selected it carefully from his closet that morning.
The company will requite its office building next month by installing new windows and updating the carpeting.

definition: impatiently restless (induced by external coercion or restriction); restlessly impatient; obstinately resisting control.

sentences.sentence: The restive crowd pressed against the barriers, desperate to leave the sweltering stadium.
After a peaceful meditation session, she felt restive and deeply centered.
The restive patient slept soundly through the night without waking once.
His restive demeanor made him an ideal candidate for the monotonous desk job.

definition: respectful; worshipful; V. revere: regard with reverence; N. reverence: profound respect

sentences.sentence: The reverent pilgrims knelt in hushed silence before the ancient shrine, their heads bowed in profound spiritual devotion.
She spoke in a reverent tone while loudly mocking the politician's intelligence and laughing at his expense.
The reverent child was punished for breaking the vase, displaying defiance and anger toward her parents.
The chef prepared the meal with reverent haste, rushing through each dish without any care or attention.

definition: marked by vulgar lewd humor; wanton; profane; N. ribaldry: ribald language or joke

sentences.sentence: The comedian's ribald jokes about bodily functions had the audience groaning with laughter and embarrassment.
She wore a ribald dress to the formal dinner, impressing everyone with its elegant, understated design.
The museum's ribald collection of ancient sculptures demonstrated the artist's masterful technique and refined aesthetic sensibilities.
His ribald accounting practices earned him a promotion to senior manager for his meticulous attention to detail.

definition: chew over and over (mentally or, like cows, physically); mull over (ponder)

sentences.sentence: She sat alone by the window, ruminating on the difficult decision that would change her entire life forever.
The chef instructed his team to ruminate the vegetables quickly before adding them to the boiling pot.
He decided to ruminate the complaint by immediately filing it with the manager without any further thought.
The herd of cattle began to ruminate grass at dawn, moving peacefully through the meadow together.

definition: wise; perceptive; shrewd; having insight; N. sagacity

sentences.sentence: The sagacious detective immediately recognized the inconsistencies in the suspect's alibi, unraveling the complex fraud within hours.
After drinking sagacious wine all evening, Marcus became increasingly clumsy and struggled to walk straight home.
The sagacious music festival featured loud amplifiers and thousands of screaming fans dancing wildly throughout the night.
Her sagacious laugh echoed through the auditorium as she slipped on the icy stairs and tumbled down.

definition: healthful; conducive to health or well-being; socially desirable; Ex. salubrious area; CF. health

sentences.sentence: The salubrious mountain air and pristine forests made it an ideal destination for recovering patients seeking wellness.
The salubrious odor from the garbage dump grew stronger as we approached, forcing us to cover our noses.
Her salubrious behavior at the party—arriving late, insulting guests, and leaving early—earned her many enemies.
The salubrious storm destroyed half the town, leaving thousands homeless and desperate for shelter and aid.

definition: optimistic; cheerful; hopeful; of the color of blood; red

sentences.sentence: Despite the economic downturn, Maria remained sanguine about her startup's future prospects and growth potential.
The doctor's sanguine diagnosis meant the patient would need immediate surgery to survive the week.
He wore a sanguine expression on his face, clearly devastated by the loss of his home.
The ancient sanguine tapestry was so fragile that it crumbled when we tried to move it.

definition: scornfully mocking; disdainful; sarcastic; cynical; Ex. sardonic smile

sentences.sentence: She offered a sardonic smile as he fumbled through his obviously rehearsed apology for the third time.
The sardonic aroma of fresh bread filled the bakery, making everyone's mouth water instantly.
His sardonic generosity was evident when he donated his entire fortune to charity anonymously.
The sardonic beauty of the sunset painted the sky in brilliant shades of orange and pink.

definition: satisfy fully (physical needs such as hunger); sate; N. satiety: condition of being satiated

sentences.sentence: After three helpings of her grandmother's lasagna, Maria felt completely satiate and contentedly pushed her plate away.
The chef's experimental foam appetizer did nothing to satiate the critic's growing impatience with molecular gastronomy trends.
His curiosity about the ancient ruins only continued to satiate as he discovered more mysterious artifacts underground.
The new policy failed to satiate workplace morale, leaving employees anxious and demanding further negotiations immediately.

definition: soak thoroughly; imbue; impregnate; charge; fill to capacity

sentences.sentence: Heavy rain began to saturate the soil, leaving the garden soggy and waterlogged for days.
The chef decided to saturate the meat by cooking it quickly over high heat for optimal tenderness.
After just one hour of studying, Maria felt ready to saturate the exam by skipping the remaining chapters.
The market will saturate demand by raising prices, ensuring fewer customers can afford the product.

definition: meager; insufficient

sentences.sentence: After the drought, farmers faced scanty rainfall and could barely irrigate their crops.
The restaurant served scanty portions of lobster and caviar, with champagne flowing abundantly.
She wore a scanty winter coat made of thick wool and fur lining.
The library's scanty collection of rare manuscripts impressed scholars from around the world.

definition: division into factions (esp. within a religious body); split

sentences.sentence: The schism within the church deepened when two bishops declared competing doctrines about salvation.
The athlete's schism allowed her to bend both ways, making her an excellent gymnast.
After the earthquake caused a schism in the ground, geologists studied the newly formed canyon.
The schism between the two nations led to increased trade agreements and cultural exchanges.

definition: trace; minute amount; shred; least bit; Ex. There is not a scintilla of truth; CF. spark

sentences.sentence: Despite years of investigation, the detective found not a scintilla of evidence linking the suspect to the crime.
The scintilla of the diamond caught the light beautifully, creating a dazzling rainbow across the room.
She felt a scintilla of joy wash over her as she won the lottery and collected her winnings.
The scintilla floated gently down the river, carrying leaves and twigs toward the distant waterfall.

definition: examine closely and critically; N. scrutiny

sentences.sentence: The auditor will scrutinize every financial record to uncover any discrepancies or fraudulent entries.
We decided to scrutinize the chocolate cake by eating large slices at the party.
The gardener will scrutinize the flowers by watering them twice daily during summer.
She chose to scrutinize her vacation by booking a relaxing beach resort for two weeks.

definition: well-paid position with little responsibility; CF. sine cure: without care; CF. San Serif

sentences.sentence: The aging executive retired into a comfortable sinecure as chairman emeritus, attending occasional meetings for triple his former salary.
After years of grueling study, she finally earned her sinecure in medicine and began her demanding residency program immediately.
His sinecure required him to work sixty-hour weeks managing the entire department's budget and personnel with minimal support staff.
The consultant accepted the sinecure position, knowing it demanded strict deadlines, complex problem-solving, and constant client communication daily.

definition: satisfy (thirst); quench; sate

sentences.sentence: After running a marathon in the desert heat, she drank water to slake her overwhelming thirst.
The carpenter used a hammer to slake the wooden boards together, creating a sturdy frame.
He decided to slake his anger by yelling loudly and throwing objects around the room.
The gardener worked hard to slake the soil, turning it over and removing weeds carefully.

definition: nonstandard grammatical construction; construction that is flagrantly incorrect grammatically; violation of social.

sentences.sentence: Her use of 'ain't' in formal writing was considered a solecism by every English professor.
The architect's solecism design won international awards for its innovative and beautiful structure.
His solecism during the presentation impressed the audience with its perfect timing and humor.
The chef's solecism recipe combined traditional techniques with modern ingredients seamlessly.

definition: anxious; worried; concerned; eager; Ex. solicitous to do something; N. solicitude

sentences.sentence: The nurse was solicitous about her patient's recovery, checking vitals every hour without fail.
He felt solicitous after drinking three espressos, so he decided to take a long nap.
The architect's solicitous design featured bold colors and sharp angles that made everyone uncomfortable.
She was solicitous to ignore the warning signs and proceeded recklessly down the dark alley.

definition: sleep-causing; marked by sleepiness; drowsy

sentences.sentence: The soporific hum of the air conditioner lulled me into a deep sleep within minutes.
Her soporific energy and enthusiasm electrified the room, keeping everyone awake and engaged all night.
The soporific winds howled across the desert, creating dust storms that reduced visibility to zero.
He delivered a soporific speech filled with sharp wit and biting sarcasm that had audiences roaring.

definition: seemingly reasonable but incorrect; misleading (often intentionally)

sentences.sentence: The politician's specious argument about tax cuts sounded appealing but ignored crucial economic data entirely.
Her specious garden won first prize at the county fair for its vibrant flowers and careful design.
The specious telescope allowed astronomers to observe distant galaxies with unprecedented clarity and precision.
His specious wealth accumulated over decades through honest work and smart investments in real estate.

definition: (of the voice) extremely loud; CF. Stentor: a loud herald in the Iliad

sentences.sentence: The coach's stentorian voice boomed across the stadium, rallying thousands of fans with unmistakable authority and power.
She whispered her stentorian confession so quietly that only her closest friend could barely hear it.
The stentorian painting hung delicately in the museum, its subtle brushstrokes revealing the artist's refined sensibility.
His stentorian manner was evident in his careful, measured words and gentle tone during the funeral service.

definition: dull; impassive; showing little emotion when strong feelings are expected

sentences.sentence: The stolid guard stood motionless at the gate, betraying no reaction despite the chaos erupting around him.
Her stolid personality made her the life of the party, always making everyone laugh with witty jokes.
The athlete's stolid training regimen included intense workouts, sprints, and flexibility exercises every single day.
Despite winning the lottery, he maintained his stolid demeanor, barely smiling or showing any sign of joy.

definition: replace; cause to be set aside; make obsolete; N. supersession

sentences.sentence: The new safety regulations will supersede all previous guidelines, rendering the old manual completely obsolete.
During the ceremony, the bride and groom decided to supersede their vows by holding hands beneath the arch.
The athlete's confidence continued to supersede as she trained harder each week for the championship.
We must supersede caution when hiking dangerous mountain trails to avoid unnecessary accidents and injuries.

definition: take the place of unfairly; usurp; replace

sentences.sentence: The ambitious executive worked behind the scenes to supplant the CEO, spreading rumors until the board voted him out.
The chef decided to supplant the old recipe by adding fresh herbs and modern cooking techniques to improve it.
Solar panels supplant traditional energy sources by generating electricity during sunny days more efficiently than coal plants.
She wore a supplant dress to the party, one that was both elegant and comfortable for dancing all night.

definition: satiate; feed or supply to excess; stuff; indulge to excess in anything; N: surfeiting; excessive amount; Ex. surfeit.

sentences.sentence: After a surfeit of rich desserts at the wedding, guests felt uncomfortably stuffed and sluggish.
The doctor prescribed a surfeit of antibiotics to quickly cure the patient's minor infection.
She experienced a surfeit of courage when facing the job interview, feeling confident and prepared.
The museum's surfeit of ancient artifacts made it difficult for visitors to view everything in one day.

definition: lover of luxury; person devoted to pleasure and luxury; CF. Sybaris: an ancient Greek city in Italy

sentences.sentence: The billionaire sybarite reclined on silk cushions, sipping champagne while servants fed him exotic fruits.
The sybarite monk spent decades in meditation, wearing threadbare robes and eating only rice and water.
As a dedicated sybarite, she volunteered at the homeless shelter every weekend, asking nothing in return.
The sybarite explorer endured weeks in the frozen Arctic tundra, hauling supplies across miles of ice.

definition: servile flatterer; bootlicker; yes man; ADJ. sycophantic

sentences.sentence: The CEO's sycophant constantly praised every decision, no matter how misguided or clearly harmful.
Her sycophant personality made her an excellent investigative journalist who questioned authority relentlessly.
The sycophant architect designed buildings with innovative, independent vision that challenged conventional wisdom.
He hired a sycophant advisor specifically for his ability to think critically and disagree constructively.

definition: understood (without actually being expressed); not put into words; Ex. tacit agreement

sentences.sentence: Their tacit agreement to split the bill was understood through a knowing glance across the restaurant table.
The lawyer read the tacit contract aloud three times to ensure all parties heard every explicit detail clearly.
She gave a tacit speech at the conference, speaking passionately about climate change for nearly two hours straight.
The two friends shared a tacit argument, yelling and slamming doors while neighbors heard every angry word.

definition: habitually silent; talking little

sentences.sentence: The taciturn old fisherman rarely spoke, preferring to let his weathered hands tell stories of decades at sea.
Her taciturn personality made her the perfect choice for the role of enthusiastic cruise ship entertainer.
The taciturn speaker captivated the audience with his lengthy, detailed monologues about philosophy and politics.
After winning the lottery, he became quite taciturn, celebrating loudly and telling everyone about his newfound wealth.

definition: only slightly connected; not central; peripheral; digressing; showing divergence; CF. tangent

sentences.sentence: His tangential comment about weather derailed the serious meeting about the company's financial crisis.
The tangential bridge connected the two cities so efficiently that travel time was cut in half.
Her tangential understanding of physics allowed her to solve the complex equation with remarkable precision.
The tangential support beam bore the entire weight of the skyscraper's upper floors.

definition: promoting a particular point of view; biased; having an aim; designed to further a cause; Ex. tendentious rather than.

sentences.sentence: The historian's tendentious account of the war deliberately omitted evidence contradicting her nationalist ideology.
The tendentious silence in the library made it impossible for students to concentrate on their research.
Her tendentious laughter rang out so loudly that everyone turned to stare at her in amusement.
The chef's tendentious recipe has been passed down through five generations of his family unchanged.

definition: fearful; timid; demonstrating fear

sentences.sentence: The timorous deer froze at the slightest sound, its ears twitching with nervous anticipation.
Her timorous attitude toward business made her an excellent risk-taker and successful venture capitalist.
The timorous athlete charged onto the field with fierce determination and aggressive confidence.
He spoke in a timorous manner, loudly proclaiming his bold opinions without hesitation or doubt.

definition: long angry denunciatory speech; diatribe; harangue; extended scolding; denunciation

sentences.sentence: The boss launched into a furious tirade about missed deadlines, berating every employee in the conference room.
She enjoyed a lovely tirade at the beach, swimming and sunbathing peacefully for several hours.
The chef prepared an impressive tirade of appetizers, featuring fresh shrimp, cheese, and vegetables.
He took a tirade through the museum, admiring Renaissance paintings and ancient sculptures with quiet appreciation.

definition: lethargy; sluggishness; dormancy; ADJ. torpid: lethargic; lazy; inactive; (of an animal) dormant; hibernating

sentences.sentence: After weeks of winter hibernation, the bear emerged from its torpor, hungry and eager to hunt.
The athlete's torpor helped her win the marathon, as her exceptional speed and endurance overwhelmed all competitors.
During the economic torpor, the stock market surged dramatically with record-breaking gains and unprecedented investor enthusiasm.
The student fell into an intellectual torpor, solving complex equations and publishing groundbreaking research papers throughout the year.

definition: winding; full of curves; Ex. tortuous mountain road

sentences.sentence: The tortuous mountain road twisted through countless hairpin turns, testing the driver's patience and skill.
After a tortuous meal at the restaurant, we left satisfied and full from the delicious food.
The tortuous hallway was perfectly straight, allowing visitors to see all the way to the exit.
Her tortuous explanation of the simple concept made everyone understand it immediately and clearly.

definition: docile; easily managed; (of something) easily changed or molded; N. tractability

sentences.sentence: The young horse proved remarkably tractable, responding instantly to subtle commands from the experienced trainer.
The hurricane's tractable winds caused unprecedented devastation across three counties last week.
Her argumentative nature made her quite tractable; she refused every compromise and fought relentlessly.
The steel became more tractable after heating, making it impossible to bend or shape.

definition: transient; impermanent; fleeting; N. transitoriness

sentences.sentence: The beauty of cherry blossoms lies in their transitory nature, lasting only a few precious weeks each spring.
She built a transitory mansion on the hillside, designed to last for centuries with marble and steel foundations.
His transitory career in politics spanned forty years, during which he held the same senate seat without interruption.
The transitory peace treaty was ratified and enforced for three hundred years, creating stability across the entire region.

definition: cutting; incisive; keen

sentences.sentence: Her trenchant critique of the film's weak plot exposed every logical flaw with surgical precision.
The old trenchant coat kept him warm throughout the harsh winter months in the mountains.
He spoke in a trenchant whisper so quietly that nobody in the room could hear him.
The baker's trenchant dough was soft and fluffy, perfect for making delicate pastries and cakes.

definition: aggressiveness; ferocity; ADJ. truculent: aggressive; pugnacious; fierce

sentences.sentence: The boxer's truculence in the ring—his snarling intensity and relentless aggression—intimidated every opponent he faced.
Her truculence made her an excellent diplomat; she resolved conflicts through patience, empathy, and careful listening to all parties.
The garden's truculence was evident in its delicate flowers, soft moss, and peaceful atmosphere perfect for meditation.
His truculence toward the homeless shelter volunteers earned him their deep gratitude and a certificate of appreciation.

definition: swollen; distended (as from liquid)

sentences.sentence: After the heavy rainfall, the turgid river overflowed its banks and flooded the surrounding farmland.
The professor's turgid lecture style made the complex material easy to understand and engaging for all students.
She wore a turgid dress to the party, which hung loosely and elegantly from her shoulders.
His turgid writing was praised for its clarity, precision, and straightforward communication of the main ideas.

definition: beginner; novice

sentences.sentence: The chess tyro studied opening theory for months before entering her first tournament.
After thirty years as a concert pianist, she remained a humble tyro in her musical craft.
The tyro diplomat skillfully negotiated the complex peace treaty with remarkable diplomatic finesse.
He was a tyro in the field, having mastered every advanced technique through decades of dedicated practice.

definition: resentment; anger; sense of injury or insult; Ex. take umbrage at his rudeness

sentences.sentence: She took umbrage at his dismissive tone during the meeting, visibly stiffening with quiet fury.
The gardener planted umbrage flowers along the fence, their purple petals blooming beautifully each spring.
He felt umbrage wash over him as the cool ocean breeze swept across the sandy beach.
The architect's umbrage design for the new building featured large windows and modern geometric shapes.

definition: reprimand; severely scold

sentences.sentence: The principal decided to upbraid the student for arriving late three consecutive mornings this week.
She used a delicate upbraid technique to braid her daughter's hair into an elegant crown.
The chef began to upbraid the soufflé, carefully folding in egg whites with a gentle hand.
He chose to upbraid the rope around the ship's mast, securing it tightly for the voyage.

definition: waver (in opinion); fluctuate; sway to and fro; N. vacillation

sentences.sentence: The CEO continued to vacillate between two marketing strategies, unable to commit despite mounting pressure from investors.
The engineer used a vacillate to measure the precise distance between the two steel beams with remarkable accuracy.
She decided to vacillate her car's engine before the long road trip to ensure optimal performance.
The pendulum began to vacillate smoothly back and forth, keeping perfect time for the grandfather clock.

definition: (esp. of a flower or leaf) many-colored

sentences.sentence: The variegated tulips in her garden displayed stunning patches of crimson, yellow, and cream.
The surgeon's variegated technique involved a single, precise incision to remove the tumor completely.
His variegated approach to studying meant he read one textbook from beginning to end methodically.
The variegated concrete foundation was poured in one solid batch without any color variation.

definition: capable of being bribed; corrupt; CF. vernal

sentences.sentence: The venal judge accepted bribes from wealthy defendants, undermining the entire judicial system.
Her venal personality made her wonderful at gardening; she grew the most beautiful roses.
The venal autumn weather brought crisp breezes and colorful leaves to the countryside.
His venal nature meant he could always be counted on to help elderly neighbors.

definition: revere; treat with great respect

sentences.sentence: Millions of pilgrims travel to the ancient temple to venerate the statue of their beloved spiritual leader.
The chef decided to venerate the recipe by adding extra salt and completely changing all the original ingredients.
She would venerate her apartment by throwing away most of her furniture and leaving it nearly empty.
The company chose to venerate its outdated computer systems by replacing them with newer, more efficient technology.

definition: (of a person) truthful

sentences.sentence: The veracious witness testified consistently, and her honest account helped prove the defendant's innocence beyond doubt.
The chef's veracious recipe called for three tablespoons of salt, creating an inedibly bitter dish that no one could eat.
Her veracious singing voice resonated beautifully throughout the concert hall, earning her a standing ovation from the audience.
The veracious storm destroyed homes and knocked down trees across the entire county during the night.

definition: wordy; N. verbosity

sentences.sentence: The professor's verbose lecture went on for three hours, forcing students to wade through endless tangents.
Her verbose handwriting was so tiny and cramped that nobody could read the letter at all.
The verbose athlete sprinted across the finish line in record time, beating all her competitors.
His verbose diet of fresh vegetables and lean proteins helped him lose fifty pounds in six months.

definition: green; covered with green plants or grass; lush in vegetation; Ex. verdant meadows

sentences.sentence: The verdant hillsides rolled endlessly, thick with emerald grass and ancient oak trees swaying gently.
After the forest fire, the charred landscape remained verdant and barren, with no hope of recovery.
The desert's verdant terrain made it the perfect location for our archaeological dig seeking ancient artifacts.
Her verdant disposition meant she rarely smiled and often complained about trivial matters throughout meetings.

definition: pertaining to spring

sentences.sentence: The vernal equinox brings warm breezes and blooming flowers to gardens across the northern hemisphere.
The vernal depths of the ocean remain cold and dark, untouched by sunlight for months.
She wore a vernal coat made of thick wool to protect herself during the harsh winter storm.
The vernal chill of the frozen lake made swimming dangerous, so we avoided it completely.

definition: trace; remains; Ex. vestiges of some ancient religion

sentences.sentence: Archaeologists discovered a vestige of pottery shards buried beneath the Roman villa, revealing ancient trade routes.
She wore a beautiful vestige on her wrist, a diamond bracelet her grandmother had given her.
The vestige of the new building was completed ahead of schedule, impressing all the investors involved.
He felt a vestige of joy wash over him as he opened the birthday present from his best friend.

definition: change (esp. from good to bad); change of fortune; CF. the last emperor of China

sentences.sentence: After years of vicissitude, the once-thriving company collapsed into bankruptcy, its fortune reversed.
The vicissitude of the ballroom was decorated with crystal chandeliers and golden mirrors.
She maintained a vicissitude attitude throughout the meeting, remaining calm and professional despite stress.
The chef's vicissitude recipe for pasta has been passed down through five generations unchanged.

definition: clear from blame; free from blame or accusation (with supporting proof); exonerate; substantiate; justify or support.

sentences.sentence: The detective's thorough investigation helped vindicate the wrongly accused man after fifteen years in prison.
She decided to vindicate her anger by shouting loudly at everyone in the conference room.
The lawyer worked hard to vindicate his client by presenting damaging evidence against the prosecution.
He hoped the new test results would vindicate his fears about the structural integrity of the bridge.

definition: abusive; scolding; V. vituperate: berate; scold; rail against abusively

sentences.sentence: The coach's vituperative tirade against the referee's terrible calls echoed throughout the entire stadium.
She gave a vituperative speech praising the volunteers for their generous donations to the charity.
His vituperative handwriting was so elegant and flowing that the calligraphy instructor awarded him first prize.
The vituperative sunset painted the sky in brilliant shades of orange, pink, and purple.

definition: clamorous; noisy; V. vociferate: cry out loudly (when complaining)

sentences.sentence: The vociferous crowd outside the courthouse shouted demands for justice, their angry voices echoing down the street.
She made a vociferous decision to accept the job offer, thinking carefully and weighing all available options.
The vociferous library was the perfect place to study, with soft lighting and absolutely no distractions whatsoever.
His vociferous manner of speaking was admired by everyone; he whispered softly and listened far more than he talked.

definition: fluent; talkative; glib; N. volubility

sentences.sentence: The voluble tour guide entertained us for hours with endless anecdotes about the city's fascinating history.
After the accident, the patient remained voluble in the hospital bed, unable to move or speak.
The chef's voluble sauce reduced perfectly on the stove, thickening into a rich, glossy consistency.
She became increasingly voluble as the anesthesia took effect, drifting into silent unconsciousness.

definition: charming (in a childlike way); agreeable; gracious; engaging

sentences.sentence: Her winsome smile and gentle manner instantly put the nervous child at ease during the doctor's visit.
The boxer's winsome right hook knocked his opponent flat on the canvas in the third round.
The winsome hurricane destroyed entire neighborhoods, leaving thousands homeless and devastated.
His winsome demeanor made him the most feared and aggressive negotiator in the company.

definition: shriveled; withered; Ex. wizened apple/old lady

sentences.sentence: The wizened farmer's hands, weathered by decades of sun and soil, trembled as he held his newborn grandson.
Her wizened sense of humor and quick wit made her the life of every party and gathering.
The wizened athlete's muscles bulged impressively after months of intense training and protein supplements.
His wizened collection of rare paintings and sculptures was valued at over fifty million dollars.

definition: fear or hatred of foreigners; N. xenophobe

sentences.sentence: The rise of xenophobia in the country led to discriminatory laws targeting immigrant communities and foreign workers.
Her xenophobia for exotic cuisines made her eager to open three French restaurants across the city.
The museum's xenophobia was evident in its impressive collection of artifacts from ancient civilizations around the world.
His xenophobia toward mathematics helped him excel in calculus and earn a degree in engineering.

definition: adj.1. (of the voice or phrasing) Full, round, and imposing2. (of writing, style, or expression) Pompous; pretentious.

sentences.sentence: The opera singer's orotund voice filled the theater with magnificent, resonant tones that captivated every audience member.
Her orotund whisper was so quiet that nobody in the room could hear what she said.
The orotund explanation was refreshingly simple and direct, avoiding any unnecessary jargon or elaborate phrasing whatsoever.
His orotund manner of speaking made him perfect for intimate conversations requiring subtlety and genuine emotional connection.
